# Spanish review worklist — un puente al giorno

**Derived from the bank. Do not edit this file** — corrections go into the entry
JSON (or tell the editor), then this file is regenerated. LatAm-default audience: anything Peninsular-only must say so.

94 active entries · 86 need attention first · generated 2026-07-17

The five checks per entry: is the expression natural, is the meaning accurate, is
the example something a native would say, are register/regions honest, and does
the cited page actually support the entry (open the URL).

### 0001 — Estar en las nubes

- **lit.** "to be in the clouds"
- **meaning:** To be daydreaming or distracted.
- **example:** Perdona, ¿qué decías? Estaba en las nubes.
  — *Sorry, what were you saying? I was off in the clouds.*
- **register:** standard · **exam:** ok_in_exam · **regions:** mx, es, ar, co · **status:** draft
- **source to verify:** RAE (DLE) → https://dle.rae.es/nube (headword: nube) — NOT YET OPENED BY A HUMAN
- **notes:** Source spot-checked in Treccani; RAE entry not opened. Direction of warning assigned es→it on import; reviewer to confirm.

### 0002 — Estar como una cabra

- **lit.** "to be like a goat"
- **meaning:** To be a bit crazy; said jokingly or affectionately.
- **example:** Mi vecino está como una cabra, pone música a las tres de la mañana.
  — *My neighbor's nuts, he blasts music at 3am.*
- **register:** colloquial · **exam:** neutral · **regions:** es · **status:** draft
- **source to verify:** RAE (DLE) → https://dle.rae.es/cabra (headword: cabra) — NOT YET OPENED BY A HUMAN
- ⚑ **REGION:** STRONGLY PENINSULAR. Rare in Mexico — a Mexican speaker would more likely say 'está zafado' or 'está loco de remate'. Needs a LatAm equivalent before shipping to a LatAm-default audience.
- **notes:** THE CAUTIONARY ENTRY. Shipped unflagged in the personal app. IT side is slang and dictionary-absent (source is a slang dictionary, mapped to authority 'Corpus'); ES side is Peninsular-only. Both problems must be resolved, not just documented. 2026-07-17: Europass list surfaced 'essere una capra' (IT) = to be IGNORANT — a false friend of this entry's ES side (como una cabra = crazy). Same goat, different defect. Consider a cross-language note or companion entry.

### 0028 — Dar la vuelta a la tortilla

- **lit.** "to flip the omelette"
- **meaning:** To completely reverse a situation, turning it in one's favor.
- **example:** Íbamos perdiendo 2-0, pero logramos dar la vuelta a la tortilla y ganamos el partido.
  — *We were losing 2-0, but we managed to turn things around and won the match.*
- **register:** colloquial · **exam:** neutral · **regions:** es, mx · **status:** draft
- **source to verify:** RAE (DLE) → https://dle.rae.es/tortilla (headword: tortilla) — NOT YET OPENED BY A HUMAN
- ⚑ **REGION:** tortilla itself is a false friend inside the idiom: omelette in Spain, corn flatbread in Mexico — yet the idiom exists in both. Mexico more commonly says '(se) volteó la tortilla' / 'voltear la tortilla'. DLE lists the locución under 'volver la tortilla'. Reviewer to pick the LatAm-default surface form.
- **trap when producing Spanish:** 'Dar la vuelta a la tortilla' does not carry the blame-shifting accusation; a Spanish listener hears a neutral or positive turnaround. For manipulative spin Spanish reaches elsewhere (e.g. 'tergiversar').
- **notes:** Submitted by Joe 2026-07-13. Meaning divergence is the payload. Reviewer to confirm: (a) MX surface form, (b) whether ES side can also carry the negative 'spin' sense in some regions, (c) 'ribaltare la situazione' as the recommended IT alternative.

> reviewer: [ ] expression is natural  [ ] meaning accurate  [ ] example natural  [ ] register/regions right  [ ] source opened & confirms

### 0029 — Había una vez

- **lit.** "there was a time"
- **meaning:** Once upon a time — the fixed opener of fairy tales and stories.
- **example:** Había una vez una princesa que vivía en un castillo.
  — *Once upon a time there was a princess who lived in a castle.*
- **register:** standard · **exam:** ok_in_exam · **regions:** mx, es, ar, co · **status:** draft
- **source to verify:** RAE (DLE) → https://dle.rae.es/vez (headword: vez) — NOT YET OPENED BY A HUMAN
- ⚑ **REGION:** The archaic variant 'Érase una vez' (with fossilised enclitic -se) is equally canonical, especially in Spain and in print. Reviewer to confirm which form is LatAm-default for the headword.
- **trap when producing Spanish:** Italians reach for essere and produce 'era una vez' — half-camouflaged by the archaic 'Érase una vez', so it slips by, but productive Spanish existentials take haber: hay, había.
- **notes:** Submitted by Joe 2026-07-14 (as 'Habia una vez / cera una volta'; forms normalised to 'Había' and 'C'era'). CLASSIFICATION: typed idiom, not formula — the schema defines formula as a fixed SOCIAL phrase tied to an occasion (buon anno), which is why formulas require pins. A narrative opener has no occasion; it is a fixed discourse idiom. Reviewer may overrule.

> reviewer: [ ] expression is natural  [ ] meaning accurate  [ ] example natural  [ ] register/regions right  [ ] source opened & confirms

### 0031 — Ojalá

- **lit.** "God willing (from Arabic law šaʾa llāh)"
- **meaning:** I hope so; if only — a wish particle that pulls the subjunctive.
- **example:** Ojalá venga mañana.
  — *I hope she comes tomorrow.*
- **register:** standard · **exam:** ok_in_exam · **regions:** mx, es, ar, co · **status:** draft
- **source to verify:** RAE (DLE) → https://dle.rae.es/ojal%C3%A1 (headword: ojalá) — NOT YET OPENED BY A HUMAN
- **trap when producing Spanish:** Deploying ojalá to mean 'maybe' fails — Spanish splits the jobs: quizá / a lo mejor for maybe, ojalá only for wishes.
- **notes:** Submitted by Joe 2026-07-14. Etymology pair (Arabic vs Greek) is a card-front gift. Exclamative 'Magari!' = 'I wish!' worth a reviewer check as third sense.

### 0032 — O sea

- **lit.** "or be-it (subjunctive of ser)"
- **meaning:** That is; I mean — the conversational rephraser.
- **example:** Llega el viernes, o sea, pasado mañana.
  — *She arrives Friday — that is, the day after tomorrow.*
- **register:** colloquial · **exam:** neutral · **regions:** mx, es, ar, co · **status:** draft
- **source to verify:** RAE (DLE) → https://dle.rae.es/ser (headword: ser) — NOT YET OPENED BY A HUMAN
- ⚑ **REGION:** Overuse is a class-marked stereotype in both Spain (pijo) and Mexico (fresa) — fine to use, risky to lean on. Reviewer to confirm framing.
- **trap when producing Spanish:** The calque 'eso es' means 'exactly!', not 'I mean'. The rephraser is o sea (conversational) or es decir (formal).
- **notes:** Submitted by Joe 2026-07-14. The o sea→ossia near-miss (real word, wrong register) is the subtlest trap in the bank so far.

> reviewer: [ ] expression is natural  [ ] meaning accurate  [ ] example natural  [ ] register/regions right  [ ] source opened & confirms

- **meaning:** By eyeball estimate; roughly, without measuring.

  — *“How much salt do I add?” “Just eyeball it.”*

- ⚑ **REGION:** Submitted from lived Mexican usage — strongly current in MX, especially in cooking. The cubero was the barrel-maker whose trained eye gauged capacity. Bare 'a ojo' also works everywhere.
- **trap when producing Spanish:** 'A ojo y cruz' is not Spanish. Bare 'a ojo' carries it everywhere; a ojo de buen cubero adds the flavor.
- **notes:** Submitted by Joe 2026-07-16 from Mexican usage (cooking context). IT pairing and the occhio/occhio-e-croce kitchen split chosen by Claude — Italian reviewer to confirm. First entry anchored in MX lived usage rather than flagged for Peninsular skew.

(theatre, vulgar) was judged both crude and a mismatch for Italy's universal wish — correct on both counts. ### 0048 — Quien fue a Sevilla perdió su silla

- **lit.** "who went to Seville lost their chair"
- **meaning:** Leave your spot and you forfeit it — the playground law of vacated seats.
- **example:** —¡Ese era mi lugar! —Quien fue a Sevilla perdió su silla.
  — *“That was my seat!” “You snooze, you lose.”*
- **register:** colloquial · **exam:** neutral · **regions:** mx, es, ar, co · **status:** draft
- **source to verify:** Cervantes → https://cvc.cervantes.es/lengua/refranero/ficha.aspx?Par=59456&Lng=0 (headword: quien fue a Sevilla…) — NOT YET OPENED BY A HUMAN
- ⚑ **REGION:** Mexico commonly says 'el que se fue a la Villa perdió su silla' — la Villa being read as the Guadalupe basilica in CDMX, a lovely re-localisation of the rhyme. María to confirm which form fronts for the MX-default audience.
- **trap when producing Spanish:** 'Quien va a Roma pierde la poltrona' draws blank stares — the Spanish pilgrimage is to Seville (or, in Mexico, la Villa), and the seat is a plain silla.
- **notes:** Submitted by Joe 2026-07-17. IT pairing by Claude — 'chi va a Roma perde la poltrona' is my recall of the canonical twin; IT reviewer to confirm form and currency. ES origin story (the 15th-c. archbishop of Seville) left out of the note as unverified trivia — reviewer may restore if it checks out.

### 0049 — Morirse de hambre

- **lit.** "to die-oneself of hunger"

- **source to verify:** RAE (DLE) → https://dle.rae.es/morir (headword: morir) — NOT YET OPENED BY A HUMAN
- ⚑ **REGION:** Non-reflexive 'morir de hambre' exists and leans LITERAL (famine, starvation). The -se is doing the figurative work — that contrast is the entry's point.
- **trap when producing Spanish:** Dropping the -se gives 'muero de hambre' — grammatical but oddly grave, like an actual famine report. Everyday hunger wants me muero.
- **notes:** Submitted by Joe 2026-07-17. The morirse/morir figurative-literal split and the dal/di cause-preposition claim are Claude's analysis — both reviewers to confirm; the ES side especially (is bare 'muero de hambre' truly marked, or regionally fine?).
